C. R. Das and Motilal Nehru argued return to Council politics.
The younger leaders like Jawaharlal Nehru and Subhash Chandra Bose pressed for more radical mass agitation and for full independence.
When the Simon Commission arrived in India in 1928 it was greeted with slogan go back Simon.
All parties including the Congress and the Muslim League participated in the demonstration.
Irwin also announced Round Table Conference to discuss a future constitution.
The Dominion status did not satisfy the Congress leaders. The radicals within the Congress led by Jawaharlal Nehru and Subhash Chandra Bose became more assertive.
Poorna Swaraj or Complete Independence for India was accepted as the goal of Congress.
It was declared that 26 January 1930 would be celebrated as a Independence Day when people were to take a pledge to struggle for complete Independence.

Salt March or Dandi March was started by Gandhiji on 12th March 1930. They walked for 24 days to cover a distance of 240 miles, about 10 miles a day from Gandhiji’s ashram in Sabarmati to the Gujarati coastal town of Dandi.
At Dandi, Gandhiji broke the Salt law by gathering natural salt found on the seashore and boiling seawater to produce salt.
Thousands of people broke the Salt Law at various coastal areas. They manufactured salt and demonstrated in front of the government factories.
Peasants started evading taxes. They stop paying revenues and chaukidari taxes. Village officials resigned.
Forest laws were violated by peasants by entering into reserved areas to collect the wood and graze cattle.
Abdul Ghaffar Khan a devout disciple of Mahatma Gandhi was arrested by the police in April 1930, this led to demonstration by the angry crowds in Peshawar.

Colonial government got frightened and responded with the policy of brutal repression. At many places, Satyagrahis were attacked, women and children were beaten and about one lakh people were arrested.
The situation was growing worse and Gandhiji decided to call off the movement.
Gandhiji went to London in December 1931. The negotiations at the second Round Table conference in London ended in a failure.
When Gandhiji came back to India, he found that most of the leaders were put in jail. Abdul Ghaffar Khan and Jawaharlal Nehru were in jail. Congress was declared an illegal organisation
A series of measures had been imposed to prevent meetings, demonstrations and boycotts.
Gandhiji re-launched the Civil Disobedience Movement with great apprehension. For over a year, the movement continued, but by 1934, it lost its momentum.

Different social groups participated in the Civil Disobedience Movement

  • Rich landlords
  • Poor peasants
  • Business class
  • Industrial working class
  • Women
Rich landlords {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Patidars of Gujarat and the Jats of Uttar Pradesh {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Notion of Swaraj was struggle against high revenues.

Rich peasants became enthusiastic supporters of the Civil Disobedience Movement.
They organised their communities and at times forced reluctant members to participate in the boycott programmes.

However, rich peasants’ communities were deeply disappointed when the movement was called off in 1931 without the revenue rates being revised.
Hence, when the movement restarted in 1932, many of them refused to participate.
Poor peasants joined a variety of radical movements, often led by Socialists and Communists.
Congress did not support poor peasants’ ‘No Rent Campaigns’ because of their association with the rich peasants and landlords.
The relationship between poor peasants and Congress was uncertain. The participation of poor peasants was limited in the movement.
To organize business interests, Business Class formed the Indian Industrial and commercial congress in 1920 and the Federation of the Indian Chamber of Commerce and Industries (FICCI) in 1927.
The business community was led by prominent industrialists like Purshottam Das and G.D. Birla.

Prominent industrialists gave financial assistance and refused to buy or sell imported goods. For them the meaning of Swaraj was when colonial restrictions on business would end and trade & industry would flourish without constraints.

Industrial Working Classes

  • Did not participate in the movement
  • Only it was in Nagpur where the participation was seen among the workers

There were railway workers’ strike in 1930 and dock workers’ strike in 1932.
In 1930, thousands of workers in Chhotanagpur tin mines wore Gandhi caps and participated in protest rallies and boycott campaigns.
Congress did not include workers’ demands due to fear of alienation of the industrialists and division of the anti-imperialist forces.
Women participated in protest marches, manufactured salt and picketed foreign cloth and liquor shops. Many went to jails.
In urban area, women were from high caste families. In rural areas, women were from rich peasant households.
Women participated as a service to the nation and as a sacred duty of women.
For a long time, the congress was reluctant to allow women to hold any position of authority within the organisation.
Mahatma Gandhi opposed it as it will slowdown integration of society. This created a conflict and tussle between Mahatma Gandhi and B.R. Ambedkar.
The congress came to be more visibly associated with the Hindu religious nationalist groups like the Hindu Mahasabha.
Every riot deepened the distance between the two communities.
Some of the Muslim leaders demanded a separate electorate for the Muslims which was not supported by the congress leaders.
No compromise on issue of reserved seats due to strong opposition of M.R. Jayakar of Hindu Mahasabha at the All Parties Conference in 1928.

Nationalism spreads when people begin to believe that they are all part of the same nation.
It develops when people find some unity that binds them together.
Sense of collective belonging in India can also be termed as Pan India belongings.
The image of Bharat Mata was first created by Bankim Chandra Chattopadhyay.

REVIVAL OF INDIAN FOLKLORE

  • In late- nineteenth-century India, nationalists began recording folk tales sung by bards
  • They also toured villages to gather folk songs and legends
  • This was done to promote the traditional culture that had been corrupted and damaged by western forces
  • To revive the folklore Rabindranath Tagore himself collected ballads, nursery rhymes and myths, and led the movement for folk revival

A massive four-volume collection of Tamil folk tales, The Folklore of Southern India was published by Natesa Sastri. He believed that folklore was national literature; it was ‘the most trustworthy manifestation of people’s real thoughts and characteristics.’

During the Swadeshi movement in Bengal, a tricolor flag (red, green, yellow and red) was designed. It had eight lotuses representing eight provinces of British India, and a crescent moon, representing Hindus and Muslims.

By 1921, Gandhiji had designed the Swaraj Flag. It was a tricolour (white, green and saffron).
It had a spinning wheel in the centre, representing the Gandhian ideal of self-help.

Reinterpretation of history

  • Many Indians began feeling that to instill a sense of pride in the nation
  • Indian history had to be thought about differently.
  • The British saw Indians as backward and primitive, incapable of governing themselves
  • Various writers started writing about the glorious Indian past
  • They wrote about the developments that took place in the past in various categories
  • They explained how our glorious past declined with the coming of the colonial rule in our country

Agenda 21

An agenda to combat environmental damage, poverty and disease through global co-operation on common interests, mutual needs and shared responsibilities.
Earth Summit was organized in 1992 in Rio de Janeiro in which more than 100 state heads agreed with Agenda 21 for the sustainable development and proper use of resources.
Resource planning is essential for sustainable existence of all forms of life.
An equitable distribution of resources has become essential for a sustained quality of life and global peace.
It was believed that resources are free gifts of nature. As a result, human beings used them indiscriminately.
Resources are vital for human survival as well as for maintaining the quality of life.
Gandhiji thought that exploitative nature of modern technology is the root cause for depletion at global level.

Overuse of resources creates many socio-economic problems. To overcome these problems, resource conservation at various levels is important. This had been the main concern of the leaders and thinkers in the past.
India has made concerted efforts for achieving the goals of resource planning right from the First Five Year Plan launched after Independence.
There is a need for balanced resource planning at the national, state, regional and local levels.
Resource planning becomes more important in a country like India, where resources are not distributed properly.
Planning is the widely accepted strategy for judicious use of resources.
India has land under a variety of relief features, namely mountains, plateaus, plains and islands.
Land is an asset of a finite magnitude, therefore, it is important to use the available land for various purposes with careful planning.
Land supports natural vegetation, wild life, human life, economic activities, transport and communication systems.
We live on land we perform our economic activities on land and we use it in different ways. Thus, land is a natural resource of utmost importance.
Continuous use of land over a long period without taking measures to conserve and manage, degrade them. This has led to serious problem to environment.
A large population which is dwelling at the fringe of forest depends upon the forest and its produce, resulting in the reduction of forest area.

The pattern of net sown area varies widely from state to state. While net sown area is 80% in state like Punjab, it is only 10% in the state of Arunachal Pradesh, Mizoram, Manipur and Andaman and Nicobar Island.
Land other than fallow land is either of poor quality or too costly to cultivate, these lands are cultivated only once or twice in two or three consecutive years.
The total net sown area (NSA) is not more than 54% including land other than fallow land.
The land under permanent pasture is decreasing; this will create the problem for grazing.
Some areas of Jammu and Kashmir occupied by Pakistan and China has also not been surveyed.
In north eastern state, Assam is the only state to be fully surveyed for its land use.
India’s land use data is available only for 93% of the total geographical area because the land use reporting for most of the states has not been done fully.
Total geographical area of India is 3.28 million sq km.

Proper management of waste lands, control of mining activities, proper discharge and disposal of industrial effluents and wastes after treatment can reduce land and water degradation in industrial and suburban areas.
Planting of shelter belts of plants, control on over grazing, stabilisation of sand dunes by growing thorny bushes are some of the methods to check land degradation in arid areas.
In recent years, industrial effluents as waste have become a major source of land and water pollution in many parts of the country.
Mining process retards the process of infiltration of water into the soil after it settles down on the land.
The mineral processing like grinding of limestone for cement industry and calcite and soapstone for ceramic industry generate huge quantity of dust in the atmosphere.

Reasons of Land degradation
Reasons States
Deforestation due to mining Jharkhand, Chhattisgarh, Madhya Pradesh, Odisha
Overgrazing Gujarat, Rajasthan, Madhya Pradesh, Maharashtra
Over Irrigation (due to water logging leading to increase in salinity and alkalinity in the soil.) Punjab, Haryana, Western Uttar Pradesh
Human activities such as deforestation, over grazing, mining and quarrying too have contributed significantly in land degradation.
Human activities have not only brought about degradation of land but have also aggravated the pace of natural forces to cause damage to land.
Ninety-five per cent of our basic needs for food, shelter and clothing are obtained from land.
On the basis of the factors responsible for soil formation, colour, thickness, texture, age, chemical and physical properties, the soils of India are classified in different types.
Soil also consists of organic (humus) and inorganic materials.
Chemical and organic changes which take place in the soil are equally important.
Various forces of nature such as change in temperature, actions of running water, wind and glaciers, activities of decomposers etc. contribute to the formation of soil.
It takes millions of years to form soil upto a few cm in depth.
Soil is the medium of plant growth and supports different types of living organisms on the earth. The soil is a living system.
Soil is the most important renewable natural resource.
Forest soils found in the lower parts of the valleys particularly on the river terraces and alluvial fans are fertile.
In the snow-covered areas of Himalayas, forest soils experience denudation and are acidic with low humus content.
Forest soils are loamy and silty in valley sides and coarse grained in the upper slopes.
Forest soils are found in the hilly and mountainous areas where sufficient rain forests are available.
After proper irrigation arid soils become cultivable as has been in the case of western Rajasthan.
The lower horizons of the arid soil are occupied by Kankar because of the increasing calcium content downwards. The Kankar layer formations in the bottom horizons restrict the infiltration of water.
Due to the dry climate, high temperature, evaporation is faster and arid soil lacks humus and moisture.

Arid soils range from red to brown in colour.  They are generally sandy in texture and saline in nature. In some areas the salt content is very high and common salt is obtained by evaporating the water.
Lateritic soils are mostly deep to very deep, acidic (pH<6.0), generally deficient in plant nutrients.
Laterite soil has low humus content because decomposers, like bacteria, get destroyed due to high temperature.
Laterite soil is suitable for cultivation with adequate doses of manures and fertilizers.
Laterite soil is found in Karnataka, Kerala, Tamil Nadu, Madhya Pradesh, and the hilly areas of Odisha and Assam.
Laterite soil develops in areas with high temperature and heavy rainfall.
Laterite has been derived from the Latin word ‘later’ which means brick.
Red soil develops a reddish colour due to diffusion of iron in crystalline and metamorphic rocks. It looks yellow when it occurs in a hydrated form.
Yellow and red soils are found in parts of Odisha, Chhattisgarh, southern parts of the middle Ganga plain, and along the piedmont zone (foothill region) of the Western Ghats.
Red soil develops on crystalline igneous rocks in areas of low rainfall in the eastern and southern parts of the Deccan plateau.
Black Soils are rich in soil nutrients, such as calcium carbonate, magnesium, potash and lime. These soils are generally poor in phosphoric contents.
The black soils are made up of extremely fine i.e., clayey material and well-known for their capacity to hold moisture.
Black Soil covers the plateaus of Maharashtra, Saurashtra, Malwa, Madhya Pradesh and Chhattisgarh and extend in the south east direction along the Godavari and the Krishna valleys.
Black soil is typical of the Deccan trap (Basalt) region spread over northwest Deccan plateau and is made up of lava flows.
Black soils are black in colour and are also known as regur soils.
Alluvial soil is rich in potash, phosphoric acid and lime which are ideal for the growth of sugarcane, paddy, wheat and other cereal and pulse crops.
The bangar soil has higher concentration of kanker than the Khadar. Khadar has more fine particles and is more fertile than the bangar.
According to their age alluvial soils can be classified as old alluvial (Bangar) and new alluvial (Khadar).
Apart from the size of the grains or components, Alluvial soils are also described on the basis of their age: Bangar and Khadar
The alluvial soil consists of various proportions of sand, silt and clay. As we move inlands towards the river valleys, soil particles appear somewhat bigger in size.

Entire northern plains are made of alluvial soil. Alluvial soil is also found in the eastern coastal plains particularly in the deltas of the Mahanadi, the Godavari, the Krishna and the Kaveri rivers.
India has varied relief features, landforms, climatic realms and vegetation types. These have contributed in the development of various types of soils.

Shelter belts: Planting lines of trees lead to shelter of crops. Rows of such trees are called shelter belts. These shelter belts have contributed significantly to the stabilisation of sand dunes and in stabilising the desert in western India.
Strip cropping: Large fields can be divided into strips. Strips of grass are left to grow between the crops. This breaks up the force of the wind. This method is known as strip cropping.
Terrace farming: Steps can be cut out on the slopes making terraces. Terrace cultivation restricts erosion. Western and central Himalayas have well developed terrace farming.
Contour ploughing: Ploughing along the contour lines can decelerate the flow of water down the slopes. This is called contour ploughing.
Soil erosion is caused due to defective methods of farming. Ploughing in a wrong way i.e. up and down the slope form channels for the quick flow of water leading to soil erosion.
Wind blows loose soil off flat or sloping land known as wind erosion.
Sometimes water flows as a sheet over large areas down a slope. In such cases the top soil is washed away. This is known as sheet erosion.
The land becomes unfit for cultivation and is known as bad land. In the Chambal basin such lands are called ravines.
The running water cuts through the clayey soils and makes deep channels as gullies.
Soil Erosion by Human activities: Deforestation, over-grazing, construction and mining etc., contributes in soil erosion
Soil Erosion by Natural ways: Wind, glacier and water lead to soil erosion
Removal of top soil is called soil erosion. The denudation of the soil cover and subsequent washing down is described as soil erosion.

topics.topic_name: Conservation of Forest and Wildlife in India

cards.text: Conservation in the background of rapid decline in wildlife population and forestry has become essential.
Conservation preserves the genetic diversity of plants and animals for better growth of species and breeding. For example, in agriculture, we are still dependent on traditional crop varieties.
In the 1960s and 1970s, conservationists demanded a national wildlife protection programme.
The Indian Wildlife (Protection) Act was implemented in 1972, with various provisions for protecting habitats. An all-India list of protected species was also published.
The thrust of the programme was towards protecting the remaining population of certain endangered species.
The central government has announced several projects for protecting specific animals which were greatly threatened.

The projects include protection of animals such as the tiger, the one-horned rhinoceros, the Kashmir stag or hangul, the three types of crocodiles-the freshwater crocodile, the saltwater crocodile and the Gharial, the Asiatic lion and others.

Most recently, the Indian elephant, black buck (chinkara), the great Indian bustard (godawan) and the snow leopard, etc. have been given full or partial legal protection against hunting and trade throughout India.
The conservation projects are focusing on biodiversity rather than on a few of its components.
Insects are included in the list of conservation planning.
In the notification under Wildlife Act of 1980 and 1986, several hundred butterflies, moths, beetles, and one dragonfly have been added to the list of protected species.
In 1991, for the first-time plants were added to the list of protected species, starting with six species.

topics.topic_name: Types and Distribution of Forest and Wildlife Resources

cards.text: It is quite difficult to maintain, manage, and control vast forest and wildlife resources, even if citizens and government desire to conserve them.
In India, much of its forest and wildlife resources are either owned or managed by the government through the Forest Department or other government departments.

Reserved Forest

  1. More than half of the total forest land has been declared reserved forests. 
  2. Reserved forests are regarded as the most valuable as far as the conservation of forest and wildlife resources are concerned.

Protected Forests 

  1. Almost one-third of the total forest area is protected forest, as declared by the Forest Department. 
  2. This forests land are protected from any further depletion.

Unclassed Forests
These are other forests and wastelands belonging to both government and private individuals and communities.

Forests are home to some of the traditional communities, in some areas of India, local communities along with government officials are struggling their best to conserve the forests and habitats of wild life recognizing that only this will secure their own long term livelihood.
In Sariska Tiger Reserve in Rajasthan, villagers have fought against mining by citing the Wildlife Protection Act.
In many areas, villagers themselves are protecting habitats and rejecting any government involvement.

The inhabitants of five villages in the Alwar district of Rajasthan have declared 1,200 hectares of forest as the Bhairodev Dakav ‘Sonchuri’, declaring their own set of rules and regulations which do not allow hunting and are protecting the wildlife against any outside encroachments.

topics.topic_name: Water Scarcity and Water Conservation and Management

cards.text: Water Availability on Earth Three-fourth of the earth's surface is covered by water, but only a small proportion of it is suitable for human use.
Freshwater Sources Freshwater is mainly obtained from surface runoff and groundwater which are renewed through the hydrological cycle.
Water Scarcity Despite water being a renewable resource, scarcity arises due to over-exploitation, excessive use, and unequal access.
Stereotypes of Water Scarcity Water shortages are often associated with low-rainfall regions like Rajasthan, visualized by women carrying water over long distances.

Causes of Water Scarcity
  • Large and growing population increasing demand for water.
  • Over-exploitation for agricultural development.
  • Unequal access and control over water resources.
Over-exploitation for Agriculture Irrigated agriculture demands excessive water use, often leading to falling groundwater levels and affecting water availability.
Impact of Industrialisation Rapid industrial growth increases pressure on freshwater resources, demanding both water and hydroelectric power.

Urbanization Effects
  • Dense populations increase water and energy demands.
  • Housing societies utilize groundwater, depleting resources.
Atal Bhujal Yojana (Atal Jal) Implemented in water-stressed regions to promote conservation and smart water management through community participation.
Water Quality Concerns Availability does not equate to usability; pollution from domestic, industrial, and agricultural sources can defeat water sufficiency.
Jal Jeevan Mission (JJM) Aims to provide rural households with a regular supply of potable piped water, enhancing living conditions.

Government Initiatives
  • Atal Bhujal Yojana focuses on behavioral change towards water management.
  • Jal Jeevan Mission strives for sustainable potable water supply.
Consequences of Mismanagement Mismanagement and over-exploitation can lead to reduced water availability and ecological degradation.
Need for Conservation Urgent need to conserve and manage water resources to ensure health, food security, livelihoods, and ecosystem stability.
Ecological Crisis If water resources are over-exploited and mismanaged, it could result in an ecological crisis affecting human life.

topics.topic_name: Multi-Purpose River Projects and Integrated Water Resources Management

cards.text:
Water Conservation Techniques in Ancient India
Archaeological evidence indicates a rich history of water conservation techniques in India using structures like dams, reservoirs, and canals for irrigation.

Ancient Hydraulic Structures
  • Sophisticated water harvesting system at Sringaverapura near Allahabad (1st century BC).
  • Extensive construction of dams and lakes during Chandragupta Maurya's time.
  • Advanced irrigation systems discovered in Kalinga, Nagarjunakonda, and Kolhapur.
  • Bhopal Lake built in the 11th century was a significant artificial lake.
  • Hauz Khas tank in Delhi, constructed in the 14th century, was for water supply.

Functions of Dams
Dams serve multiple purposes like irrigation, electricity generation, water supply, flood control, and recreation, making them multipurpose projects.

Integrated Water Resources Management
Post-independence, multi-purpose projects were intended to drive national development, integrate agricultural and industrial growth, and overcome colonial legacies.

Types and Classifications of Dams
  • Structure-based: Timber, embankment, and masonry dams.
  • Height-based: Large, major, low, medium, and high dams.

Challenges and Impacts of Dams
Dams face scrutiny due to potential negative effects like altered river flow, reduced habitat quality, migration challenges for aquatic fauna, vegetation submersion, and sedimentation issues.

Sardar Sarovar Dam
Built over the Narmada River, it provides irrigation to numerous drought-prone regions in Gujarat, Rajasthan, and Maharashtra.

Flood Risks and Mismanagement
Dams can trigger floods when sediment build-up in reservoirs prevents effective water management during heavy rains.

Environmental Consequences of Dams
Dams can lead to ecological issues such as soil salinization, silt deprivation in floodplains, land degradation, and increased water-borne diseases.

Pradhan Mantri Krishi Sinchayee Yojana
This initiative aims to ensure broad access to irrigation, improve water usage efficiency, and encourage sustainable conservation practices.

Impacts on Agriculture
Irrigation has changed cropping patterns towards water-intensive crops resulting in soil salinization and ecological effects.

Krishna-Godavari Dispute
The dispute involves Maharashtra, Karnataka, and Andhra Pradesh over water diversion by Maharashtra, affecting downstream agriculture and industry.

Role of Dams in Ecosystems
In addition to providing utilities, dams can alter ecosystems by affecting river and sediment flow, impacting aquatic life and floodplain environments.

Sedimentation Concerns
Excessive sedimentation restricts water flow and, coupled with lack of silt, leads to agricultural degradation.

Displacement and Resettlement Issues
Large dam projects often lead to the displacement of communities who need resettlement, which can pose social and economic challenges.

Traditional Water Harvesting
Ancient Indians developed sophisticated techniques to harvest rainwater, groundwater, river, and flood water according to local ecological conditions.

Western Himalayas Techniques
In hill regions, diversion channels known as 'guls' or 'kuls' were built for agriculture.

Rooftop Rainwater Harvesting in Rajasthan
This method was predominantly used to collect and store drinking water.

Flood Plains of Bengal
Inundation channels were created to irrigate fields.

Rajasthan's Arid Regions
Agricultural fields in Jaisalmer were converted into rain-fed storage structures referred to as 'khadins.'

Underground Tanks in Bikaner
In places like Phalodi, houses had large underground 'tankas' as part of a rooftop rainwater harvesting system.

Purity of Rainwater
'Palar pani' or rainwater is considered the purest form and can be stored until the next rainfall.

Shillong's Water Challenges
Despite high rainfall, Shillong faced water shortages leading households to depend on rooftop rainwater harvesting.

Decline of Traditional Practices
In western Rajasthan, the practice declined with the availability of water from the Indira Gandhi Canal.

Gendathur's Success Story
The village in Karnataka successfully adapted rooftop rainwater harvesting with 200 households embracing the technique.

Thar's Rainwater Management
Common in towns and villages, rooftop systems guide rainwater into underground 'tankas.'

Tamil Nadu's Legal Initiatives
Tamil Nadu made it compulsory for all houses to have rooftop rainwater harvesting structures.

Bamboo Drip Irrigation in Meghalaya
A traditional system using bamboo pipes directs water from springs to lower reaches, managing flow with pipe positions.

Bamboo Pipes in Action
They transport water over great distances with channels made to divert water to plant sites.

Incorporating Roads in Water Systems
If bamboo pipes cross roads, they are elevated high above the ground.
Ethnic Composition of Sri Lanka
In Sri Lanka, most of the Sinhala-speaking people were Buddhists, while most of the Tamils are Hindus or Muslims.
In Sri Lanka, there were about 7 percent Christians, who were both Tamil and Sinhala.
Sinhalese enjoyed the majority and could impose their will on Sri Lanka.
Sri Lanka emerged as an independent country in 1948.
In Sri Lanka, the leaders of the Sinhala community sought to secure dominance over government by virtue of their majority.

In Sri Lanka, majoritarianism policy was adopted by Sinhala people.
Majoritarianism is a belief that the majority community should be able to rule a country in whichever way it wants, by disregarding the wishes and needs of the minority.
In Sri Lanka, the democratically elected government adopted a series of Majoritarian measures to establish Sinhala supremacy.
All the majoritarian government measure to establish Sinhala supremacy increased the feeling of alienation among the Sri Lankan Tamils.
Sri Lankan Tamils felt that none of the major political parties led by the Buddhist Sinhala leaders was sensitive to their language and culture.
Sri Lankan Tamils felt that over time, tension had grown in the constitution and between the Tamil and Sinhala communities.

Sri Lankan Tamils launched parties and struggles

  • For the recognition of Tamil as an official language
  • For regional autonomy
  • For equality of opportunity in securing education and jobs.
Tamils in Sri Lanka demanded more autonomy for the Tamil-populated regions, but this request was repeatedly rejected.
By 1980s several political organisations were formed by Sri Lankan Tamils demanding an independent Tamil Eelam (state) in northern and eastern parts of Sri Lanka.
The distrust between the Sri Lankan Tamil and Sinhala communities turned into widespread conflict. It soon turned into a civil war.
Civil war is violent conflict between opposing groups within a country that becomes so intense that it appears like a war.
Thousands of people of Sri Lankan Tamil and Sinhala communities have been killed. Many families were forced to leave the country as refugees and many more lost their livelihoods.
The civil war has caused a terrible setback to the social, cultural and economic life of the Sri Lanka. Civil war ended in 2009.
In Belgium, the minority French-speaking community was relatively rich and powerful.
In Belgium, Dutch-speaking community got angry as they got the benefit of economic development and education much later.
During the 1950s and 1960s, tensions between the Dutch-speaking and French-speaking communities created due to differences.
The tension between the French and Dutch communities was more acute in Brussels.
Brussels presented a special problem: the Dutch-speaking people constituted a majority in the country, but a minority in the capital.
The Belgian leaders recognised the existence of regional differences and cultural diversities.
Belgian model is very complicated, even for people living in Belgium. But these arrangements have worked well so far.
Belgian model helped to avoid civic strife between the two major communities and a possible division of the country on linguistic lines.
When many countries of Europe came together to form the European Union, Brussels was chosen as its headquarters.

Need of Power Sharing

  • Prudential Reasons
  • Moral Reasons
Power sharing helps in reducing the conflict between various social groups. For example, seats in legislative assemblies and parliament have been reserved for SCs and STs.
Power sharing is necessary for maintaining social harmony and peace.
Power sharing is good way to ensure political stability in the country.

In the long run, enforcing the will of the majority group on the minority may weaken the nation's unity. For example, lakhs of people from both communities were killed in Sri Lanka's civil war between Tamils and Sinhala.
Power sharing is the very spirit of democracy.
A democratic rule involves sharing power with those affected by its exercise, and who have to live with its effects.
People have a right to be consulted on how they are to be governed.
A legitimate government is one where citizens, through participation, acquire a stake in the system.
Horizontal Division of Power Sharing
In Horizontal Division of power sharing, power is shared among different organs of the government like Legislature, Executive and Judiciary.
Under horizontal distribution of power, organs of the government are placed at the same level to exercise different powers.

The Horizontal Division of power-sharing specifies the concept of check and balance. Each organ has its own power and checks the powers of others. This results in the in a balance of power among various institutions. This arrangement is called a system of checks and balances.
Under horizontal distribution of power, all the organs work at the same level but for different purpose.
VERTICAL DIVISION OF POWER SHARING

POWER SHARING AMONG SOCIAL GROUPS
Power may be shared among different social groups such as the religious and linguistic groups.
In some countries, there are constitutional and legal arrangements whereby socially weaker sections and women are represented in the legislatures and administration.

In India, there is a system of reserved constituencies. In a diverse country; like India; there are various social, linguistic and caste groups and power is shared among each group. For example; people from the minority communities, OBCs, and SC & ST are given reservation.

Power sharing among different social groups is meant to give proper share in the government and administration to diverse social groups. If proper share is not given to such communities, they would feel alienated from the government. Hence, this method is used to give minority communities a fair share in power.
Community Government in Belgium is a good example of Power sharing among different social groups.
In a democracy, power is also shared among different political parties.

Democracy provides the citizens a choice to choose their rulers. This choice is provided by the various political parties, who contest elections to win them. Such competition ensures that power does not remain in one hand.
Power is shared between various political parties through coalition government in case a single largest party is not winning majority.
In a democracy interest group includes traders, businessmen, industrialists, farmers and industrial workers.
The interest group will have a share in governmental power, either through participation in governmental committees or bringing influence on the decision-making process.

Coming Together

  • Independent states coming together on their own to form a bigger unit.
  • The individual states have sole authority equal power.
  • By pooling sovereignty and retaining identity; they increase their security.
  • Example: U.S.A., Switzerland, Australia
Two kinds of routes through which federations have been formed

The exact balance of power between the central and the state government varies from one federation to another. This balance depends mainly on the historical context in which the federation was formed.

Two aspects of an ideal federal system

  1. Mutual trust
  2. Agreement to live together

Dual objectives
The federal system thus has dual objectives:

  • To safeguard and promote unity of the country.
  • At the same time accommodate regional diversity.
Financial autonomy: Sources of revenue for each level of government are clearly specified to ensure its financial autonomy.

Same Citizens, Separate jurisdiction:
Different tiers of government govern the same citizens, but each tier has its own jurisdiction in specific matters of legislation, taxation and administration.

Supreme authority of the courts:
Courts have the power to interpret the constitution and the powers of different levels of government. The highest court acts as an umpire if disputes arise between different levels of government in the exercise of their respective powers.

Two or more levels of government:
Federalism is a system of government in which the governmental power is divided between a central authority and its various constituent units.

Features of Federalism

  • Two or three tiers of government
  • Existence and authority of each tier of govt is constitutionally guaranteed
  • Each tier has their own judiciary
  • Changes brought down by the consent of both the levels of government
  • Financial Autonomy
  • Dual objectives
  • Independent Judiciary

Though only 25 of the world's 192 countries have federal political systems, their citizens make up 40 per cent of the world's population. Most of the large countries of the world are federations. However, China the most populous country of the world and one of the biggest does not practice Federalism.

Unitary Government
Either there is only one level of government or the sub-units are subordinate to the central government.
The central government can pass on orders to the provincial or the local government.
The second level of governments are at the level of provinces or states or at the local. Such government look after much of the day-to-day administering of their state.
The first level of government is for the entire country. Such government is usually responsible for a new subject of common national interest
Federations has two or three levels of government
Federalism is a system of government in which the power is divided between a central authority and various constituent units of the country.
Sri Lanka continues to be a unitary system where the national government has all the powers. Tamil leaders want Sri Lanka to become a federal system.
Belgium shifted from a unitary to a federal form of government.
In 1993, the regional governments were given constitutional powers that were no longer dependent on the central government.

Regional governments existed in Belgium before 1993. Regional governments had their roles and powers. But all the powers were given to these governments and could be withdrawn by the Central Government.
In the Constitution of Belgium, the major key change was made. It was the reduction of the power of the Central Government and to give the powers to the regional governments.

Consent of both the levels of government:
The fundamental provisions of the constitution cannot be unilaterally changed by one level of government. Such changes require the consent of both levels of government.

topics.topic_name: What make India a Federal Country

cards.text: The Union and State governments have the power to raise resources by levying taxes in order to carry on the government and the responsibilities assigned to each of them.
In case of any dispute about the division of powers, the High Courts and the Supreme Court make a decision.
The judiciary plays an important role in overseeing the implementation of constitutional provisions and procedures.

Making amendments in power sharing arrangement

  • The sharing of power between the Union Government and the State governments is basic to the structure of the Constitution.
  • The Parliament cannot on its own change this arrangement.
  • Any change to it has to be first passed by both the Houses of Parliament with at least two-thirds majority.
  • Then it has to be ratified by the legislatures of at least half of the total States.

Union Territories

  • There are some units of the Indian Union which enjoy very little power.
  • These are areas which are too small to become an independent State but which could not be merged with any of the existing States.
  • Areas like Chandigarh, or Lakshadweep or the capital city of Delhi, are called Union Territories.
  • These territories do not have the powers of a State. The Central Government has special powers in running these areas.
Indians who are not permanent residents of this State cannot buy land or house in Assam, Nagaland, Arunachal Pradesh and Mizoram as per Article 371.

As per Article 371, states such as Assam, Nagaland, Arunachal Pradesh and Mizoram enjoy special powers in relation to the protection of land rights of indigenous peoples, their culture and also preferential employment in government services.

States such as Assam, Nagaland, Arunachal Pradesh and Mizoram enjoy special powers under certain provisions of the Constitution of India (Article 371) due to their peculiar social and historical circumstances.

Residuary List
Subjects which are not present in Union List, State List or Concurrent list are known as Residuary Subjects.
Union Government has the power to legislate on these subjects.
These subjects came into being after the Constitution was created.
Examples: Computer software, E-Commerce etc.
The Constitution clearly provided a threefold distribution of legislative powers between the Union Government and the State Governments. Thus, it contains three lists:

The Constitution originally provided for a two-tier system of government, the Union Government/Central Government, representing the Union of India and the State governments. Later, a third tier of federalism was added in the form of Panchayats and Municipalities.
The Constitution declared India as a Union of States. Although the constitutions did not use the word federation, the Indian Union is based on the principles of federalism.
India had emerged as an independent nation after a painful and bloody partition. Soon after Independence, several princely states became a part of the country.

Indian Constitution did not give the status of national language to any one language. Hindi was identified as the official language. But Hindi is the mother tongue of only about 40 percent of Indians. Therefore, there were many safeguards to protect other languages.
Language Policy: After creation of linguistic States, a second test for Indian federation was the language policy.
States like Nagaland, Uttarakhand and Jharkhand were created not on the basis of language but to recognise differences based on culture, ethnicity or geography.

In 1947, the boundaries of several old States of India were changed in order to create new States. Many states were created on the basis of language to ensure that people who speak the same language lived in the same state.
Linguistic States: The creation of linguistic States was the first and a major test for democratic politics.

Practice of Federalism in India

  • Linguistic states
  • Language policy
  • Centre - state relations
The nature of democratic politics ensured that the spirit of federalism, respect for diversity and desire for living together became shared ideals in our country.
The real success of federalism in India can be attributed to the nature of democratic politics in our country.
Besides Hindi, there are 21 other languages recognised as Scheduled Languages by the Constitution.

According to the Constitution, the use of English for official purposes was to stop in 1965. The central government responded by agreeing to continue the use of English along with Hindi for official purposes.
Promotion does not mean that the Central Government can impose Hindi on states where people speak a different language.
Federalism has been strengthened in practise through restructuring the relationship between the Center and the States.

Situation during Congress Monopoly
For a long time, Congress ruled both at the Centre and in most of the States.
The parties that ruled at the Centre tried to undermine the power of the states.
The Central Government has been misusing the Constitution to dismiss the State governments
This undermined the spirit of federalism.
In 1990 there was the rise of regional political parties in many States of the country.

The year 1990 was the beginning of the era of Coalition Government at the Centre. Since no single party got a clear majority in the Lok Sabha, the major national parties had led to enter into an alliance with many parties.
Coalition Government: lead to a new culture of power sharing and respect for the autonomy of State Government.
Decentralisation: When some power is taken away from the central and state governments and given to local governments.
A major step towards decentralisation was taken in 1992. The Constitution was amended to make the third-tier of democracy more powerful and effective.
Governance Structure

Gram Panchayat

  • Each village, or a group of villages in some States, has a gram panchayat.
  • This is a council consisting of several ward members, often called panch, and a president or sarpanch.
  • They are directly elected by all the adult population living in a village or ward.
  • Gram Panchayat is the decision-making body for the entire village.

Gram Sabha

  • The Panchayat works under the overall supervision of the Gram Sabha.
  • All the voters in the village are its members.
  • It meets at least twice or thrice in a year to approve the annual budget of the Gram Panchayat and to review its performance.

Panchayat Samiti/Mandal

  • When Gram Panchayats are grouped together, they form a Panchayat Samiti or Block or Mandal.
  • The member of Panchayat Samiti representative are elected by all the Panchayat members in that area.

Zila Prishad

  • All the Panchayat Samitis or Mandals in a district together constitute the Zila (district) Parishad.
  • Members of the Lok Sabha, MLAs of the district, some other officials of other district-level bodies are its members.

Urban local self-government

  • Municipalities are set up in towns.
  • Big cities are constituted into municipal corporations.
Municipalities and Municipal Corporations are controlled by elected bodies consisting of people’s representatives.
The Municipal Chairperson is the political head of the Municipality. In a Municipal Corporation such an officer is called the Mayor.

topics.topic_name: What Development Promises - Different People Different Goals

cards.text:
Understanding Development
Development is about contemplating various ways to reach our goals. It's a complex task that includes a multidisciplinary approach, involving economics, history, and political science.

Significance of Historical Context
Our current way of life is shaped by historical influences. Any desire for change requires awareness of past events.

Democratic Process
Democratic political processes are essential to realizing hopes and possibilities in real life.

Goals of Landless Rural Labourers
  • Increased work days and better wages
  • Quality education for children
  • Abolition of social discrimination
  • Opportunities for leadership in the village

Goals of Prosperous Farmers from Punjab
  • Higher family income through support prices and affordable labor
  • Desire to settle children abroad

Goals of Farmers Depending on Rain
These farmers likely seek reliable water resources and improved irrigation techniques.

Urban Unemployed Youth
They aim for access to job opportunities and skill development programs.

Expectations of a Rich Urban Girl
  • Equal freedom as her brother
  • Research options of studying abroad
  • Opportunity to decide her future path

Adivasi Aspirations
An adivasi community may seek sustainable development that preserves their land and way of life.

Development and Conflicting Interests
Different individuals may have diverse goals, leading to conflicts. For example, a girl's quest for equality may contrast with her brother's traditional views, while large dams for electricity can disrupt tribal lands.
  • Development for one can be destructive for another.

Importance of Income The desire for regular work, higher wages, and better prices for goods is often driven by the need for increased income. Income serves as a fundamental requirement for fulfilling material needs.

Value Beyond Income Many people prioritize equal treatment, freedom, security, and respect. These non-material goals can surpass the significance of income, as they contribute to a better quality of life.

Role of Non-Material Aspects Life quality is influenced by friendships and respect. They provide intrinsic value that cannot be easily quantified, similar to other non-material aspects often overlooked but crucial.

Irrelevance of Measurement in Importance The inability to easily measure non-material aspects does not make them any less important. Considerations about job offers encompass factors beyond income, reflecting their significance.

Considerations for Job Selection When choosing jobs, factors such as family facilities, work atmosphere, and opportunities for learning are important in addition to salary. Security in employment plays a vital role in job selection.
Security vs. High Pay A high-paying job without security might be less desirable compared to one offering stability and time for family, which adds to personal freedom.
Mixed Goals in Development Development encompasses more than income, including security and dignity. Women's work engagement can improve their social standing, highlighting a mix goal approach.
Impact of Women's Respect Respect for women leads to shared housework and increased acceptance of women working outside, fostering a supportive social environment.
Contribution of Safe Environments Safe and secure environments encourage women to pursue diverse careers or entrepreneurship, expanding economic activities.
Broader Developmental Goals Developmental goals include both income improvement and non-material aspirations, representing what individuals truly value for a fulfilling life.

Concept of National Development
The idea of national development can vary widely among individuals due to their distinct personal goals and perspectives.

Diverse Perspectives
Individuals may share different views, ideas, and suggestions regarding national development due to varied backgrounds and experiences.

Classroom Discussion
Engage in discussions to understand diverse opinions on India's development strategies:
  • Listen to different viewpoints.
  • Compare and contrast ideas presented.

Varied Answers
Expect a range of answers from students during discussions on development, indicating the subjective nature of this concept.

Uncertainty in Perspectives
It is common to grapple with many answers and uncertainty when considering the best path for national development.

Possible Conflicts
Understand that varied notions might not only be different but also in conflict with each other, reflecting diverse priorities and needs.

Importance of Open Dialogue
Foster an environment where open dialogue is encouraged to better appreciate the multifaceted dimensions of national development.

topics.topic_name: How to Compare Different Countries or States?

cards.text:
Comparison
Comparing different things involves identifying both similarities and differences. For instance, individuals vary in height, health, talents, and interests.

Development and Criteria
Development can have different meanings. The criteria used for comparison depend on purpose, such as choosing sports, debate, or music teams.

Characteristics for Comparison
Comparison is usually based on important characteristics, though there can be different opinions on what is considered important.

Income as a Development Indicator
Income is a key attribute for comparing countries, with higher income indicating higher development, suggesting greater access to desired resources.

Limitations of Total Income
Total income differs due to varying populations, thus not effectively indicating the average individual's earnings.

Understanding Per Capita Income
Per capita income is calculated by dividing a country's total income by its population, providing a clearer measure of average earnings.

Equitable Distribution
Even with identical average incomes, country A is preferred over country B because income distribution in A is more equitable.

Case Study: Two Countries
Country A and Country B have the same average income, but vastly different income distribution. Most citizens in A have similar incomes, whereas one person in B holds most wealth.

Impact of Income Distribution
While average income is useful, it does not reflect income distribution, which affects true living conditions.

Choosing Preferences
Given a choice, many prefer living in a country like A due to equitable income distribution rather than a country with vast income disparities but the same average income.

Individual and Collective Goals
People not only focus on income but also value security, respect, equal treatment, and freedom. Nations think similarly beyond average income.

Per Capita Income
The per capita income is a rough estimate of average earnings of individuals in a state. It helps to gauge economic development.

Comparison of State Incomes
Haryana, Kerala, and Bihar have varying per capita incomes with Haryana leading, Kerala in the middle, and Bihar at the bottom for 2021-22.

Infant Mortality Rate (IMR)
IMR indicates the number of children dying before age one per 1000 live births. It reflects a region's healthcare quality.

Rate and Literacy
Table shows literacy rates, with Kerala at 94%, Haryana at 82%, and Bihar at 62%. Literacy improves economic and social well-being.

Net Attendance Ratio
This measures school attendance among children aged 15-17 years. Kerala has 94%, Haryana 73%, and Bihar 69% attendance.

Significance of Infant Mortality Rate
Kerala records lowest IMR at 6, reflecting better healthcare compared to Haryana's 28, illustrating health development disparities.

Socio-Economic Context
Despite economic growth, issues like infant mortality arise, depicting socio-economic challenges 70 years post-Indian independence.

Educational Disparities
Educational access varies as Bihar's one-third 15-17-year-old students do not attend secondary school.

Beyond Income
Development requires weighing other criteria like education and mortality rates, as income alone doesn't capture quality of life.

Public Facilities and Collective Provision Money in your pocket cannot buy all essential goods and services needed for a good living, such as a pollution-free environment or disease prevention. These often need collective provision.
Education and Accessibility The availability of public schools and facilities is essential for education. Some areas lack adequate facilities, hindering education accessibility, especially for girls.
Infant Mortality Rate in Kerala Kerala's low Infant Mortality Rate is attributed to adequate provisions for basic health and educational facilities.
Public Distribution System Well-functioning Public Distribution Systems in some states likely improve health and nutritional status.

Educational Achievement in Uttar Pradesh Table 1.5 shows a literacy rate of 76% for rural males and 54% for females. Children aged 10-14 have a higher literacy rate, with 85% of boys and 82% of girls attending school.
Human Development Report: Understanding Growth Income alone cannot measure development. Health and education indicators also play a critical role.
Comparative Health and Education Indicators The Human Development Report uses health, education, and income indicators for comparing development stages.
Income vs. Life Expectancy According to Table 1.6, despite India's higher income level than Nepal and Bangladesh, those countries have a higher life expectancy.
HDI Rankings Table 1.6 reveals HDI rankings: Sri Lanka ranks 78, while India stands at 134. The Human Development Index (HDI) incorporates health, education, and income levels.
Focus on Human Development The Human Development Report emphasizes the importance of well-being and health for the citizens of a country in development.

Understanding Development Sustainability
The concept of development sustainability emphasizes maintaining development levels for future generations while ensuring that current development practices are sustainable.

Threats to Groundwater in India
- Overuse is a pressing issue in India.
- 300 districts reported a decline in water levels over 20 years.
- 1/3 of the country overuses groundwater, potentially increasing to 60% in 25 years.
- Key areas impacted include agriculturally rich regions and urban settlements.

Renewable vs. Non-Renewable Resources
  • **Renewable Resources:** Naturally replenished like groundwater, but still at risk of overuse.
  • **Non-Renewable Resources:** Fixed stock on Earth, as exemplified by crude oil, cannot be replaced once exhausted.

Crude Oil Reserves and Sustainability
- Global crude oil reserves are diminishing, estimated to last 50 more years.
- Differences in reserves between regions explain geopolitical tensions.

Impact of Oil Dependency
- Countries with low reserves depend on imports, making them vulnerable to price fluctuations, affecting national economies.

Environmental Degradation
- Environmental degradation consequences transcend borders, impacting global sustainability.
- The interconnected nature of sustainability necessitates international cooperation.

Global Efforts in Sustainability
Multidisciplinary efforts involving scientists, economists, and philosophers focus on resolving sustainability challenges.

Strategic Oil Reserves Data
  • **Middle East:** 836 Thousand Million Barrels (70 years)
  • **USA:** 69 Thousand Million Barrels (10.5 years)
  • **World:** 1732 Thousand Million Barrels (47 years)

Impacts of Oil Price Fluctuations
- Price changes affect import-dependent countries like India, creating economic burdens.
- The USA's approach may include using power to secure resources.

Ongoing Debate on Development
Development entails continual reassessment within societies about future goals and progress pathways, ensuring alignment with sustainability.

chapters.chapter: ECO Sectors of the Indian Economy
chapters.category_name: ECO Sectors of the Indian Economy

cards.text:
Definition of Economic Sectors

Economic activities are grouped into sectors based on specific criteria used for classification. Each sector represents a key area of the economy.

Primary Sector

This sector involves activities that require direct use of natural resources. Examples include agriculture, dairy, fishing, and forestry. The end products from these activities are natural products like cotton, milk, and minerals.

Characteristics of Primary Sector
  • Dependent on natural factors like sunshine and rainfall.
  • Provides the base for all other products.

Secondary Sector

Encompasses activities that transform natural products into new forms through manufacturing associated with industrial activities. This sector is also known as the industrial sector.

Examples of Secondary Activities
  • Using cotton fibre to create yarn and fabric.
  • Manufacturing sugar from sugarcane.
  • Producing goods in factories or workshops.

Tertiary Sector

This sector includes activities that support the primary and secondary sectors. They generate services instead of goods and are thus also called the service sector.

Examples of Tertiary Activities
  • Transporting goods from production sites to markets.
  • Banking and financial services to facilitate trade.
  • Communication such as telephones and letters for business.

Service Sector Details

Besides aiding production, the service sector includes essential services such as healthcare, education, and administration. Emerging services include IT-based services like internet cafes and call centers.

Interdependence among Sectors

The sectors are interdependent. For instance, the industrial sector relies on raw materials from the primary sector, while both depend on the service sector for support.

Impact of Sectoral Changes

Changes in one sector can affect others. If farmers don't sell sugarcane, sugar mills may shut. If transport services strike, urban food supplies become scarce.

Understanding the Three Sectors The economy comprises three sectors: primary, secondary, and tertiary. These sectors are involved in extensive production activities, contributing varied goods and services. Each sector employs a wide range of people required for production.

Assessing Sector Dominance In any economy, a single sector or multiple sectors might dominate in terms of production and employment. This dominance varies based on the size, nature, and value of goods and services produced.

Valuing Goods and Services Instead of counting quantities, economists suggest using the value of goods and services. E.g., meaning of wheat at Rs 20 per kg translates to Rs 2,00,000 for 10,000 kgs. This approach allows comprehensive economic analysis.

Differentiating Final and Intermediate Goods Final goods reach consumers, while intermediate goods are used in production. For accurate GDP calculations, only the value of final goods is considered, avoiding double-counting.
Constructing GDP Total production in each sector adds up to form a country's GDP, reflecting the value of all final goods and services produced annually, indicating the economy's size.
Measuring GDP in India India's central government assesses GDP with state and union territory departments by collecting data on goods volume and prices, estimating national GDP effectively.
Transition from GDP to GVA India shifted to calculating the contribution of sectors via Gross Value Added (GVA), aligning with global practices. GVA accounts for taxes and subsidies adjustments.

Historical Sectoral Shifts Initially, the primary sector was predominant in developed countries. With advancements, secondary and eventually tertiary sectors gained prominence, illustrating fundamental economic development patterns.

From Agriculture to Industry As agriculture advanced, additional job opportunities emerged, leading to increased roles in crafts, trading, and administration. New manufacturing methods further expanded factory jobs.

The Rise of Services In the last 100 years, there's been a notable shift to the tertiary sector, especially in developed nations. Today, this service sector is vital for employment and production, reflecting modern economic evolution.

topics.topic_name: Primary Secondary and Tertiary Sectors in India

cards.text: Production Growth Over Four Decades Graphical data from 1977-78 and 2017-18 demonstrates significant production growth across primary, secondary, and tertiary sectors. The most notable increase is observed in the tertiary sector.

Tertiary Sector Dominance The tertiary sector in India surpassed the primary sector by 2017-18, becoming the largest producing sector. This was driven by:
  • Growing demand for basic services (health, education, policing).
  • Service needs stemming from agriculture and industry development.
  • Rising income levels influencing more service demand.
  • Emergence of information and communication technology services.

Employment Patterns and GVA Despite shifts in GVA distribution, primary sector remains the largest employer. Secondary and tertiary sectors, while productive, have not parallelly increased employment opportunities.
Underemployment Indicators Many agricultural workers, such as farmer Laxmi, face disguised unemployment. Workers produce below their potential due to insufficient employment opportunities.

Opportunities for Rural Employment Increasing rural employment requires multi-faceted approaches:
  • Irrigation projects and credit facilities for enhanced agricultural output.
  • Infrastructure investments (transportation and storage).
  • Promotion of semi-rural industries (dal mills, storage, honey centers).

Implications for the Education Sector Increased demand for education results in potential job creation:
  • More teachers and educational buildings needed.
  • Potential to generate 2 million jobs in the education sector.
Health Sector Development Improving health services necessitates more professionals in rural areas. This sector offers opportunities for large-scale employment growth.

Regional Economic Potential Economic growth can capitalize on regional strengths:
  • Tourism can potentially create 3.5 million jobs annually.
  • Craft industries and IT services need planning and support.
MGNREGA 2005: Employment Rights The Mahatma Gandhi National Rural Employment Guarantee Act ensures 100 days of employment to rural families. Unemployment allowance is provided if this cannot be met.
Short-term Initiatives To create immediate employment, short-term measures complement long-term strategies. Ongoing projects are prioritized to increase productivity from land.

topics.topic_name: Division of Sectors as Organized and Unorganized

cards.text: Kanta's Work Conditions - Works in an office from 9:30 a.m. to 5:30 p.m. - Receives regular salary, provident fund, medical and other allowances. - Enjoys paid holidays and has a formal appointment letter with terms and conditions.

Kamal's Work Conditions - Works as a daily wage laborer in a grocery shop. - No additional allowances apart from daily wages. - No paid holidays, leave, or formal employment contract. - Employment is insecure and at the employer's discretion.

Characteristics of the Organised Sector
  • Regular employment terms: regulated by government laws like the Factories Act and Payment of Gratuity Act.
  • Assured work hours with overtime pay.
  • Benefits include paid leave, medical benefits, and retirement pensions.
  • Formal registration and adherence to rules and processes.

Characteristics of the Unorganised Sector
  • Small, scattered units with minimal government oversight.
  • Irregular, low-paid jobs with no overtime or paid leave.
  • Employment is insecure; workers can be dismissed at will.
  • Includes self-employed workers and seasonal laborers.

Sectors of Employment in Rural Areas
  • Landless agricultural laborers, small/marginal farmers, sharecroppers.
  • Support needed through agricultural inputs, credit, storage, and marketing facilities.

Sectors of Employment in Urban Areas
  • Workers in small-scale industry, construction, trade, and transport.
  • Jobs as street vendors, garment makers, and rag pickers.
  • Need governmental support for raw materials and marketing.

Support for Unorganised Sector Workers
  • Provide protection from exploitation and ensure fair wages.
  • Offer social and economic support to marginalized communities.
  • Address social discrimination and improve earning stability.

Challenges and Protections for Unorganised Sector
  • Job security is low due to economic shifts, leading workers to this sector.
  • Necessary to develop policies for providing health and social security.
  • Focus on creating more opportunities and formal structures.

Dependency on Organised Sector Jobs
  • Organised sector jobs are highly sought-after and stable.
  • Growth in these sectors is slow, leading many into the unorganised sector.
  • Some organised enterprises operate informally to avoid taxes.

Overall Economic Impact
  • Unorganised sector heavily relies on inadequate support systems.
  • Sectors need systematic investment to boost employment and economic growth.
  • Socio-economic development is crucial for supporting vulnerable communities.

topics.topic_name: Sectors in Terms of Ownership - Public and Private Sectors

cards.text:
Public Sector Overview
The public sector is characterized by government ownership of assets. It provides essential services to the populace.
  • Examples include railways and the post office.

Private Sector Overview
In the private sector, private entities own assets and services are provided for profit.
  • Examples are TISCO and Reliance Industries Limited.

Motive in Private Sector
The primary objective of private sector activities is profit generation. Customers pay to receive services from companies and individuals.

Objective in Public Sector
Unlike the private sector, the public sector does not prioritize earning profits. Governments raise and allocate funds to provide services.

Infrastructure and Costs
Public sector projects, such as building roads and bridges, involve substantial investments that the private sector might not afford.

Government Support for Businesses
Government interventions include supporting industries by subsidizing electricity costs, facilitating continued production and business sustainability.

Agricultural Support
The government procures wheat and rice at fair prices to sell at reasonable rates, ensuring support for both farmers and consumers.

Essential Services and Government Responsibility
The government is responsible for delivering core services like healthcare and education to the population, especially elementary education.

Human Development Concerns
Addressing human development aspects such as malnutrition and infant mortality rates is crucial. This involves providing safe drinking water and housing.

Focused Government Spending
Increased government spending is necessary to improve conditions in underprivileged and neglected regions, thus uplifting them.

topics.topic_name: Money as a Medium of Exchange

cards.text:
Concept: Everyday Transactions
Money is involved in numerous daily transactions.
  • Goods buying and selling.
  • Services exchanged for money.
  • Promises to pay money later.

Concept: Flexibility with Money
Holding money allows for easy exchange for any desired commodity or service.

Example: Shoe Manufacturer's Need
The manufacturer sells shoes for money, then uses that money to buy wheat.

Challenge: Barter System
A barter requires double coincidence of wants, meaning two parties need to want what the other offers.

Double Coincidence Issues
  • Both parties must agree to sell and buy each other's goods.
  • Without money, trades are complex and less efficient.

Role of Money in Exchange
Money eliminates the double coincidence necessity by acting as an intermediate step.

Efficiency with Money
  • The shoe manufacturer can sell shoes to any buyer.
  • After receiving money, he can purchase wheat without needing to directly find a mutually interested farmer.

Conclusion: Medium of Exchange
Money simplifies transactions by serving as an easily exchangeable intermediary.

Barter System Description
Involves direct exchange of goods without money, requiring mutual exchange interests.

Benefits of Money
Money's use enhances fluidity in the economy by preventing complicated barter trades.

Historical Use of Money
In early ages, Indians used grains and cattle for transactions before coins were invented.

Transition to Metallic Money
Metallic coins such as gold, silver, and copper were utilized after grains and cattle.

Modern Currency
The term "modern currency" refers to paper notes and coins, devoid of intrinsic value.

Government Authorization of Currency
Currency is accepted as a medium of exchange because it's authorized by the government.

Role of Reserve Bank of India
The Reserve Bank of India issues currency notes on behalf of the central government.
  • Legally, no other party can issue currency.
  • The law supports rupee as a medium of payment that can't be refused.

Bank Deposits
People deposit additional cash in banks at the end of each month, availing interest and ensuring safekeeping.

Demand Deposits
Deposits that can be withdrawn anytime are called demand deposits and function as a medium of exchange.

Cheque Payment Mechanism
Cheques enable individuals to make payments without using actual cash, facilitating bank transfers.
  • Example: M. Salim settles dues to the leather supplier through a cheque.
  • The cheque instructs M. Salim's bank to pay the specific amount to the supplier's bank.

Importance of Cheques
Cheques are pivotal as they ensure payments directly from bank accounts, bypassing cash completely.

Role of Banks in Modern Money
Banks are integral to modern forms of money—currency and deposits—due to their function in facilitating transactions.

Cash Reserve Ratio
Banks maintain a small percentage, such as 5% in India, of their deposits as cash for unexpected withdrawals by depositors.

Provisions for Withdrawals
This cash reserve acts as a provision to pay depositors withdrawing money on a given day.

Loan Extension
Most of the deposits are used to extend loans, fulfilling the heavy demand for loans for various economic activities.

Economic Mediation
Banks mediate between people with surplus funds and those needing funds by utilizing deposits for loans.

Difference in Interest Rates
Interest rate on loans is higher compared to the rate offered on deposits. This difference is the bank's main income source.

Demand for Loans
Loan demand is tied to economic activities requiring funding, which the banks meet using deposits.

Bank's Earning Mechanism
Banks earn primarily through the interest difference, contributing to their profitability.

Depositor and Borrower
Depositors provide funds through deposits, and banks lend these funds to borrowers needing them.

Managing Cash
Even if withdrawals are frequent, the limited cash reserve suffices due to the probabilistic nature of withdrawals.

Mediating Role of Banks
Banks bridge the financial needs of individuals with surplus and deficit by efficiently managing deposits.

Understanding Credit
Credit refers to an agreement where the lender provides money, goods, or services in return for a promise of future repayment.

Role of Credit
Credit is integral to many daily transactions and affects both individual and business finances.

Positive Impact: Festival Season Example
Salim, a shoe manufacturer, used credit to hire more workers and buy raw materials, resulting in profit and timely completion of orders during the festival season.

Salim's Credit Sources
Salim obtained credit by: 1. Buying leather on credit from the supplier. 2. Receiving an advance payment from a trader for part of the shoe order.

Positive Role of Credit
In Salim's case, credit facilitated production costs and boosted earnings, demonstrating its positive role in aiding business growth.

Negative Impact: Swapna’s Problem
Swapna, a small farmer, took a loan for cultivation, but pest damage led to crop failure and inability to repay the loan.

Swapna's Financial Crisis
Efforts with expensive pesticides failed, resulting in a debt that grew over time and forced Swapna to sell part of her land.

Debt-Trap Explanation
In some cases, credit can entrap individuals in growing debts, especially when external issues like crop failure occur.

Factors Affecting Credit Success
Credit's helpfulness depends on risk assessment and available support in case of unforeseen losses, as illustrated by both Salim’s and Swapna’s stories.

Citing the Critical Role of Credit
Credit's dual nature as either a facilitator in increasing earnings or a catalyst for debt illustrates its complex influence on individual financial situations.

Terms of Credit

Credit terms include the interest rate, collateral, required documentation, and method of repayment.

  • Interest rate: The cost of borrowing money expressed as a percentage.
  • Collateral: An asset to secure the loan.
  • Documentation: Proof like employment records and salary.
  • Repayment: Conditions regarding how and when the money is returned.

Common Types of Collateral

Collateral can include a variety of assets, such as:

  • Land and property titles
  • Vehicles
  • Livestock
  • Bank deposits

Megha's House Loan Example

Megha took a Rs 5 lakh loan at 12% annual interest to repay over 10 years.

  • She used her house papers as collateral.
  • Required to submit employment and salary documents.

Varying Terms Across Lenders

Terms of credit vary based on the lender and borrower's nature. Two examples:

  • Shyamal borrowed from a moneylender.
  • Arun received a lower interest bank loan.

Shyamal's Credit Terms

Shyamal borrows from a trader at 3% per month for farming.

  • Traders supply inputs on credit.
  • Promise to sell crops at harvest ensures repayment and profit to traders.

Arun's Bank Loan

Arun obtained a bank loan at 8.5% annually.

  • The loan can be repaid after selling part of the harvest.
  • Plans to store crops and apply for further credit.

Rama's Employment-Based Loans

Rama relies on her employer for loans at a 5% monthly interest.

  • Relies on credit for daily expenses and emergencies.
  • Repayment by working for the landowner.

Unique Credit Options in Sonpur

Rama finds it hard to get loans elsewhere; landowner-employers are the main credit sources for landless people.

Despite poor treatment, she continues with her employer for credit accessibility.

Loans from Cooperatives

Cooperatives are significant in rural credit, operating by pooling members' resources.

  • Multiple members contribute resources.
  • Can provide varying types of loans.

Krishak Cooperative System

An example of a cooperative is the Krishak Cooperative with over 2300 members.

  • Provides extensive loans for agriculture and household needs.
  • Loans revolve as repayments allow for renewed lending opportunities.

Types of Sector Credit
  • Formal Sector Loans: Includes banks and cooperatives.
  • Informal Sector Loans: Includes moneylenders, traders, employers, relatives, and friends.

Role of Reserve Bank of India
  • Supervises formal loan sources.
  • Ensures banks maintain a minimum cash balance.
  • Guides banks to lend to diverse borrowers.

Informal Sector Characteristics
  • No supervision over credit activities.
  • Lenders set any interest rate.
  • Prone to using unfair means for repayment.

Cost of Informal Loans
The interest rate is higher, leading to a larger part of earnings spent on repayment, potentially causing debt traps.

Benefits of Increasing Formal Sector Loans
  • Promotes higher incomes.
  • Allows for cheap borrowing for diverse needs.
  • Critical for national development.

Formal vs Informal Credit Distribution
  • Poor urban households receive 54% loans from informal sources.
  • Rich urban households get 83% loans from formal sources.

Credit Needs in Rural Areas
  • Formal sector meets only half of the rural credit needs.
  • Rest are fulfilled by informal sources.

Ensuring Equitable Access to Loans
Formal lending should be equally distributed, favoring the rich currently, to benefit the poor as well.

Challenges with Informal Loans
  • High interest rates do not enhance borrowers' income.
  • Indispensable for banks and cooperatives to increase lending.

Impact on Industrial Growth
Cheaper loans can encourage business setups, small-scale industries, and trades boosting economic growth.

topics.topic_name: Self Help Groups for the Poor

cards.text:
Informal Credit Sources
Poor households depend on informal credit sources such as moneylenders.
  • Moneylenders know the borrowers personally, facilitating loans without collateral.
  • High interest rates and no transaction records are common issues.

Barriers to Bank Loans
